original_abstract:
While Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S) chemistry offers a thermodynamically promising route for high-density energy storage, its practical application is hindered by sluggish kinetics. Although a variety of catalytic materials have been developed to enhance Li-S battery performance, the fundamental mechanisms of this catalysis—specifically the classic reduction of reaction barriers—remain poorly understood. Critical questions persist regarding which specific step limits the reaction rate, how catalysts influence this step under lean electrolyte conditions, and how catalytic activity is sustained once solid products encapsulate the catalyst surface. 

In this work, we identify the potential-limiting step (PLS) of Li-S batteries and demonstrate that its catalysis adheres to classical principles: the catalyst lowers the kinetic barrier of the PLS to accelerate the reaction without altering the final product composition. Through a rigorous electrochemical analysis under lean electrolyte conditions, we propose a revised reaction pathway: solid S₈ is sequentially reduced to molecular Li₂S₈ and Li₂S₄, followed by the potential-limiting reduction of Li₂S₄ into a solid Li₂S₂-Li₂S composite with a consistent 1:4 stoichiometry. Notably, we find that the traditionally cited solid-to-solid conversion from Li₂S₂ to Li₂S does not occur, and that the recharging process is inherently faster than discharging. We further show that supported cobalt phthalocyanine molecules effectively catalyze this PLS. Crucially, even after the active sites are buried by Li₂S₂/Li₂S precipitates, the system exhibits a self-catalytic effect that sustains the discharge process.
humanized_abstract:
Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S) chemistry is considered to be a promising candidate for high-energy density batteries. Unfortunately, the kinetics is a major barrier to hinder the realization of Li-S batteries. Despite numerous attempts on modifying Li-S batteries with various catalysts, the fundamental catalytic role and thereby the underlying battery functionality and mechanism remains poorly understood. Key unknowns include identifying the rate-determining step (RDS), how the catalysts affect the RDS under different lean electrolyte conditions and how the catalyst maintains high catalytic efficiency in the presence of sulfur precipitates on the catalyst surface.

We here reveal the potential-limiting step (PLS) in Li-S batteries and that its catalysis follows the classical concept that a catalyst reduces the activation energy of the rate-determining step (RDS) to facilitate the reaction without changing the product composition. Based on a detailed electrochemical investigation conducted under lean electrolyte conditions, we for the first time revealed a new reaction mechanism of solid S 8 being reduced into intermediate Li 2 S 8 and Li 2 S 4 successively, and then the PLS of Li 2 S 4 being reduced into solid Li 2 S 2 -Li 2 S composite with a consistent 1:4 stoichiometry. The solid-to-solid conversion from Li 2 S 2 to Li 2 S was not confirmed, and the charging rate is always smaller than the corresponding discharging rate. More interestingly, supported cobalt phthalocyanine molecules can act as a catalyst for the PLS. Even the catalytic sites are occluded by the precipitated Li 2 S 2 -Li 2 S composite, the battery can still sustain the discharge process.

document.output:
Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S) chemistry is considered to be a promising candidate for high-energy density batteries. Unfortunately, the kinetics is a major barrier to hinder the realization of Li-S batteries. Despite numerous attempts on modifying Li-S batteries with various catalysts, the fundamental catalytic role and thereby the underlying battery functionality and mechanism remains poorly understood. Key unknowns include identifying the rate-determining step (RDS), how the catalysts affect the RDS under different lean electrolyte conditions and how the catalyst maintains high catalytic efficiency in the presence of sulfur precipitates on the catalyst surface.

We here reveal the potential-limiting step (PLS) in Li-S batteries and that its catalysis follows the classical concept that a catalyst reduces the activation energy of the rate-determining step (RDS) to facilitate the reaction without changing the product composition. Based on a detailed electrochemical investigation conducted under lean electrolyte conditions, we for the first time revealed a new reaction mechanism of solid S 8 being reduced into intermediate Li 2 S 8 and Li 2 S 4 successively, and then the PLS of Li 2 S 4 being reduced into solid Li 2 S 2 -Li 2 S composite with a consistent 1:4 stoichiometry. The solid-to-solid conversion from Li 2 S 2 to Li 2 S was not confirmed, and the charging rate is always smaller than the corresponding discharging rate. More interestingly, supported cobalt phthalocyanine molecules can act as a catalyst for the PLS. Even the catalytic sites are occluded by the precipitated Li 2 S 2 -Li 2 S composite, the battery can still sustain the discharge process.
document.input:
While Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S) chemistry offers a thermodynamically promising route for high-density energy storage, its practical application is hindered by sluggish kinetics. Although a variety of catalytic materials have been developed to enhance Li-S battery performance, the fundamental mechanisms of this catalysis—specifically the classic reduction of reaction barriers—remain poorly understood. In this work, we identify the potential-limiting step (PLS) of Li-S batteries and demonstrate that its catalysis adheres to classical principles: the catalyst lowers the kinetic barrier of the PLS to accelerate the reaction without altering the final product composition.
